skill.name: network-security-setup
skill.description: Configure Claude Code sandbox network isolation with trusted domains, proxy rules, and safe environment variable handling.
skill.summary: Design and validate least-privilege network access policies for AI coding sandboxes.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, environment access, weak crypto, and heuristic risks, but review found these are mostly markdown examples and security guidance rather than runnable code. The skill is safe to publish with warnings because it asks users to configure network allowlists, proxy settings, local config files, and validation commands, which can weaken isolation if applied too broadly.

medium_findings.title: Network Allowlist Configuration Can Expand Sandbox Access
medium_findings.description: The skill provides trusted-domain and custom network policy examples, including public registries, CDNs, internal domains, and proxy settings. This is legitimate for a network security skill, but broad wildcard domains or proxy exceptions can reduce sandbox isolation if copied without review. Static network findings are true positives as configuration guidance, not evidence of covert network calls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly define allowed domains, wildcards, proxy settings, and local bypass rules. The risk is contextual misconfiguration, not malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Environment Secret Handling Requires Careful User Review
medium_findings.description: The skill discusses API keys, database passwords, private keys, AWS secrets, DATABASE_URL, and .env.local handling. The guidance tells users not to place secrets in sandbox configuration, so the static secret findings are not confirmed as exfiltration. Risk remains because users may provide real secret names or values while generating configuration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly names secret classes and local environment files, while also warning against storing secret values in config. This supports a medium operational risk rather than a high malicious finding.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Flagged as Code Execution
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick detections are false positives from markdown formatting and command examples. The skill file does not contain executable Ruby code or an automation script; it shows validation commands such as package install, git clone, curl, and local development commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are inside markdown documentation and are presented as manual validation steps. No evidence found of dynamic command execution logic.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak cryptography findings on description and configuration lines do not correspond to MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or insecure cryptographic implementation. They appear to match ordinary text inside a security setup guide.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited areas found network policy wording, integration references, and usage examples, not cryptographic code. Confidence is high but not perfect because the analyzer pattern details were not provided.

low_findings.title: Threat Examples Describe Attacks to Prevent
low_findings.description: References to data exfiltration, malicious registries, internal network scanning, and attacker domains are threat-model examples. They instruct users to block these behaviors rather than perform them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context labels these items as threats and mitigations. No evidence found that the skill asks the model to conduct scanning or exfiltration.

content.value_statement: AI coding tools need network access for packages, docs, and builds, but broad access can expose projects to data leakage. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design restricted domain allowlists, proxy settings, and safe environment variable practices.
actual_capabilities: Recommends trusted, no-network, or custom network isolation modes for different project contexts.
Builds domain allowlists for package registries, source control hosts, CDNs, and internal services.
Documents corporate proxy and no-proxy settings for enterprise sandbox configurations.
Separates safe configuration variables from secrets that should stay outside sandbox settings.
Provides validation steps to confirm trusted access works and untrusted domains are blocked.
limitations: Does not enforce network controls by itself; users must apply settings in their environment.
Cannot verify whether a company domain or proxy is safe without local policy review.
May need updates when package registries, CDNs, or internal service names change.
Does not replace secret management, endpoint security, or formal network governance.

use_cases.title: Open Source Development Sandbox
use_cases.description: Create a limited allowlist for public package registries, GitHub, and required CDNs while blocking unknown destinations.

use_cases.title: Enterprise Proxy and Registry Setup
use_cases.description: Plan access for internal registries, documentation sites, proxy servers, and selected public services.

use_cases.title: Sensitive Project Isolation
use_cases.description: Choose no-network or minimal custom access for repositories that contain confidential code or regulated data.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Network Mode
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a Claude Code sandbox network mode for my project. I use GitHub and npm, and I do not need internal services.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Trusted Domain List
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a trusted domain allowlist for a Node and Python project that needs GitHub, npm, PyPI, and a staging API domain.

prompt_templates.title: Review Secret Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this list of required environment variables and separate safe configuration from secrets that must stay out of sandbox settings.

prompt_templates.title: Design Enterprise Policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a custom sandbox network policy for an enterprise project with an internal npm registry, internal docs, a corporate proxy, and public GitHub access.

output_examples.input: A developer needs npm and GitHub access but wants all other network destinations blocked.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends trusted network mode, lists only required registry and source control domains, and adds checks for blocked unknown domains.

output_examples.input: A team provides API keys, database URLs, and feature flags as required environment variables.
output_examples.output: The skill separates non-sensitive flags from secrets, documents required secret names, and warns against placing values in sandbox configuration.

output_examples.input: A company uses an internal registry and a corporate proxy for all outbound development traffic.
output_examples.output: The skill proposes custom access rules for internal services, proxy routing, no-proxy exceptions, and validation steps for package installation.

best_practices: Start with no network or trusted mode, then add only domains required for the current workflow.
Use exact domains and ports when possible, and review every wildcard before applying it.
Document secret names without values, and keep secret values in approved secret stores or ignored local files.
anti_patterns: Do not allow broad wildcard domains just to avoid troubleshooting a failed build.
Do not place API keys, database passwords, private keys, or cloud secrets in sandbox configuration.
Do not skip validation that untrusted domains are blocked after changing network policy.

faq.question: Does this skill change my sandbox settings automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides configuration guidance and validation steps that a user must review and apply.

faq.question: Can it support enterprise proxy requirements?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for proxy URLs, no-proxy lists, internal registries, and selected public services.

faq.question: Does it allow secrets in sandbox configuration?
faq.answer: No. It tells users to document required secret names and keep secret values outside sandbox settings.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Can it verify that every domain is safe?
faq.answer: No. Users must confirm domain ownership, business need, and security policy before applying access.

faq.question: When should I use no-network mode?
faq.answer: Use it for sensitive projects, offline work, or reviews where package downloads and external calls are not required.
